content.assessment: Viewed through a demographic lens, the status-quo path leaves the selective-track network structurally insulated from the depopulation shock that is destabilizing the comprehensive-school network. Because 6/8-year gimnáziums are concentrated in urban/county-seat locations, they are largely shielded from the sharpest enrollment contraction, while ordinary schools—especially small ones—absorb the cohort decline and face closure. Without the proposal (the counterfactual), the same spatial insulation and cohort decline would continue on their current trajectory; monitoring changes the information available but not the underlying population dynamics. The planning horizon implied by 'monitoring' is short/reactive relative to a multi-decade fertility decline that is already restructuring the school network irrespective of tracking policy.
opportunities: Could be paired with geographically disaggregated monitoring to reveal whether track insulation widens over the projection period as birth-rate decline continues
strengths: Monitoring can generate data on how cohort decline interacts differentially with tracked vs. comprehensive schools over time
Avoids introducing new structural disruption on top of an already severe demographic shock (270 schools at risk of closure)
threats: Continued birth-rate decline (17,000 fewer one-year-olds than four-year-olds) will keep concentrating pressure on comprehensive and small-settlement schools while tracks remain largely unaffected, widening the demographic asymmetry the proposal does nothing to correct
weaknesses: Does not address the structural insulation of urban/county-seat tracks from depopulation while comprehensive schools bear the closure risk
Passive stance produces no counterfactual improvement in spatial distribution of enrollment pressure
